text.title: AI-Assisted Development Practices 2026
text.summary: Tool selection matrix for AI coding assistants and structured prompt engineering rules for consistent, reviewable AI output.

rule.statement: Match AI tool to task type: Copilot for daily inline completion, Cursor for exploratory multi-file edits, Claude Code for documentation / test suites / large refactors.
rule.rationale: Each tool has a different context window, interaction model, and latency profile. Mixing them correctly multiplies productivity; using any single tool for all tasks degrades output quality.

example.note: Three-stage pipeline: Claude Code for research/planning → Cursor for implementation flow → Copilot for inline completion.

rule.statement: Structure every coding prompt with four parts: Context (stack, constraints), Task (specific action), Requirements (must-haves, edge cases), Output (expected format, file locations).
rule.rationale: Unstructured prompts produce generic code that ignores project conventions. Four-part structure anchors the model to specifics.

rule.statement: Never auto-accept AI-generated code for auth, data access, or business logic. Manual review is required for security-sensitive areas.
rule.rationale: AI can increase defect rates significantly when used without review. Treat AI as assisted driving, not full self-driving.

rule.statement: Iterate: Draft → Review → Refine prompt → Regenerate → Final review. The first AI output is rarely production-ready.
Extract relevant clauses from this file into .aidocs/constitution.md for each project. Agents make runtime decisions from the constitution, not from this reference file.
rule.rationale: This file is a 2026 snapshot and ages. The constitution is the versioned, project-specific contract that agents can cite in PRs without risking stale references.
